perturbation.original_text: Company acknowledges and agrees that the remedy at law for any breach of this NDA is inadequate and that, in additi on to monetary damages, including but not limited to special, incidental, consequential or puniti ve damages, and any other avai lable relief at law, whether based in contract, tort or otherwise, the State Bar will be entitled to specific performance, injunctive relief or any other eq uitable remedy without the need to (i) prove actual damages and/or (ii) post any bond or other security deposit in connection with such remedy.
perturbation.changed_text: Company acknowledges and agrees that the remedy at law for any breach of this NDA is inadequate. In addition to monetary damages, including but not limited to special, incidental, consequential or punitive damages, and any other available relief at law, whether based in contract, tort or otherwise, this section will determine the project's font and style specifications.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the clause regarding specific performance and injunctive relief to a section unrelated to remedies creates ambiguity. It obscures the State Bar's right to seek equitable remedies, potentially hindering their ability to enforce the NDA effectively.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts California Civil Code § 3368, which implies the availability of specific performance in cases where monetary damages are inadequate. By obscuring the reference to specific performance in its original context, the contract makes it harder to enforce this right, and to find the intended meaning.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3368 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. SPECIFIC AND PREVENTIVE RELIEF [3366 - 3424] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 1. General Principles [3366 - 3369] ( Chapter 1 enacted 1872. ) 3368. Preventive relief is given by prohibiting a party from doing that which ought not to be done. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modification contradicts legal principles by obscuring the availability of specific performance, which is implied as a remedy when monetary damages are inadequate, as suggested by California Civil Code § 3368. The original text clearly states the availability of specific performance and injunctive relief, whereas the changed text moves this clause to an unrelated section, weakening its enforceability and creating ambiguity regarding the State Bar's right to seek equitable remedies. This undermines the intended meaning, making it harder to enforce this right. Snippet 1 says 'Preventive relief is given by prohibiting a party from doing that which ought not to be done,' and this is related to specific performance, and the changed text harms the ability to obtain this relief.

perturbation.original_text: This NDA will be governed and constr ued in all respects in accordan ce with the laws of the State of California without regard to its conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This NDA will be governed and construed in all respects in accordance with the rules for document formatting.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause clearly states that California law governs the NDA. Moving this to a seemingly irrelevant section about document formatting introduces ambiguity about the governing law. It also could create the impression that no legal standards apply, which would not hold up in court.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change contradicts California Civil Code § 1646, which mandates that a contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed, or, if that isn't specified, where it was made. Removing the governing law clause creates potential conflicts in interpretation and enforcement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1646 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1646. A contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed; or, if it does not indicate a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts California Civil Code § 1646. The original text clearly states California law governs the NDA, while the changed text references document formatting, thereby omitting any reference to governing law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, "A contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed; or, if it does not indicate a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made." By removing the governing law clause, the changed text contradicts this legal mandate for contract interpretation.

perturbation.original_text: Upon submission of a proposal to the RFP, or if the State Bar so requests, whichever occurs first, Company will promptly, and at its own expense: (i ) redeliver to the State Bar all copies of the Information in Company’s possession, whether such Information is in written, electronic, digital or other form or format; (ii) destroy any a nd all analyses, compilations, studies, or other documents in any form or format that were prep ared by or for the use of Company which contain or reflect any Information; and (iii) certify such destruction to the State Bar in writing by an authorized officer of the Company who supervised such destruction, as well as the return of all copies as specified above.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon submission of a proposal to the RFP, or if the State Bar so requests, whichever occurs first, Company will promptly begin planning the office holiday party.
perturbation.explanation: This modification completely removes the important obligation to return or destroy confidential information upon termination. It replaces it with an obligation to 'begin planning the office holiday party'. The new text bears no relationship to the topic of the section, and is nonsensical.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change weakens the protection of trade secrets and confidential information because a company is expected to return this at the end of a partnership. By modifying this, the injured party cannot easily make a case that a breach was a violation of trade secrets because there's no indication the document needs to be protected, or returned at the end of the partnership.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The original text contains an obligation to return or destroy confidential information upon termination or request. The changed text completely removes this obligation, replacing it with a nonsensical one. This directly contradicts the purpose of trade secret protection laws, which aim to safeguard confidential information. Snippet 1 defines "improper means" which includes "breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy". By removing the obligation to return or destroy confidential information, the modified contract eliminates a key mechanism for maintaining secrecy, thus facilitating potential misappropriation of trade secrets as the company no longer has an explicit contractual duty to protect the information.
